title: Implementação de Fractal em FPGA Usando o Processador Soft-Core SAPHO
journal: Encontro Nacional de Modelagem Computacional— ENMC 2025
abstract: O uso de fractais no processamento e na análise de dados tem ganhado destaque em diversas áreas científicas e tecnológicas. Neste trabalho, é apresentada a implementação do fractal do Conjunto de Mandelbrot utilizando o processador SAPHO em FPGA, explorando sua arquitetura reconfigurável para viabilizar o processamento rápido e eficiente de imagens fractais em tempo real. Além disso, propõem-se otimizações no código Assembly gerado para o SAPHO, que resultam em um ganho de desempenho de aproximadamente 25% em relação à implementação padrão.

title: An Image Acquisition System Based on State Machine and Sampling ADCs
abstract: The present work reports on the development of a digital system for image acquisition which is able to process two electric signals of amplitude varying between 0 and 10 V. The system correlates both signals in a two-dimensional (2-D) histogram. and coordinates for every event are obtained from the amplitudes of two coincident signals. The hardware consists of two analog-to-digital converters (ADCs), two complex programmable logic devices (CPLDs), and one 2 MB static random access memory (SRAM), implemented in a card that is plugged into personal computers. The data acquisition rate may be as high as 1.0 106 events per second, and does not depend on the PC processor. The software code has been written in the Delphi environment using Assembly routines for the I/O bus operations. Image sizes may be chosen from 128 128 up to 1024 1024 pixels and may be viewed in color scale, with two or three-dimen-sional graphics. Images are shown to illustrate the applicability to two-dimensional position sensitive X-ray detectors.

title: A TDC-Based System for X-Ray Imaging Detectors
authors: A. F. Barbosa, H. P. Lima, Jr.
abstract: An image acquisition system is presented for use with position sensitive detectors (PSD). The system is based on a high-resolution time-to-digital converter (TDC) and a field programmable gate array (FPGA). The detectors use gas as absorbing medium and two delay lines to identify the coordinates of each detected particle. The TDC translates the time information coming from the delay lines into digital words, from which the particle position coordinates are encoded. The FPGA is responsible for processing each event, controlling the data acquisition and communicating with a personal computer. Images of 256 × 256 pixels are stored in an on-board memory, and this resolution is increased to 512 × 512 pixels by using a time multiplexing technique. The maximum data acquisition rate is 1.2 million events per second. X-ray images obtained with the system are shown, illustrating the overall performance.

title: Production and Quality Control of MWPC for the LHCb Muon System at CERN
abstract: This work describes the production and quality control of the multi-wire proportional chamber for the LHCb muon detection system. The European Laboratory of Particle Physics (CERN) is in charge of the production of 134 chambers for the inner regions of the five Muon Stations. These chambers have demanding requirements on rate capability ( 100 kHz/cm2) and efficiency ( 99%) in a short time window (20 ns), on overall response uniformity which has to be better than 30%, and on the aging properties. The technical design of the chambers is briefly presented, and the production line is described together with the quality assurance and quality control programs. This includes fabrication of the panels; wire winding, gluing, and soldering; gap uniformity control; pitch and wire tension measurement; gas leak tests; high-voltage training; and tests under intense radiation. An original method developed for gas gain uniformity measurements is described in detail, and the obtained results are shown.

title: Gas gain uniformity tests performed on multiwire proportional chambers for the LHCb muon system
journal: Nuclear Instruments and Methods in Physics Research
abstract: We present the experimental setup and the results of the gas gain uniformity tests performed as part of the quality control of the multiwire proportional chambers produced at CERN for the LHCb muon system. The test provides a relative gas gain measurement over the whole chamber sensitive area. It is based on the analysis of the pulse height spectrum obtained when the chamber is exposed to a Am radioactive source. Since the measurement is normalized to the peak of a precise pulse generator, the gain uniformity can also be evaluated among different gas gaps and different chambers. In order to cope with the specific requirements related to the relatively high number of chambers and to their varying geometry, a standalone and compact data acquisition system has been developed which is programmable at the hardware level and may be applied to many other applications requiring precise time-to-digital and analog-to-digital conversion, in correlated or non-correlated mode.

title: Three-dimensional event visualization for the ATLAS calorimeter
abstract: The ATLAS detector was commissioned with cosmic rays. For such commissioning, a number of software tools has been developed to support data analysis. Among ATLAS sub-detectors, commissioning the calorimeter system demanded a considerable effort due to its segmentation into seven detection layers, which produce more than a hundred thousand readout channels. Tasks like performance evaluation of the calorimeter, calibration and handling noisy or dead channels benefit a lot from cosmic muon track visualization, which facilitates the identification of the activated cells in the calorimeter. The coherence of the reconstructed data can be visually checked and potential problems can be detected in a easier way. This work presents a 3D visualization tool for the ATLAS calorimeter system, the CaloGeoView. The tool was built using ROOT Framework, which provides a smooth integration with analyses currently performed by the ATLAS community. The CaloGeoView structure and some applications with reconstructed data are presented. Due to its 3D graphical interface, the CaloGeoView has been providing a simple and intuitive way to analyze results of event reconstruction.

title: Calorimeter Response Deconvolution for Energy Estimation in High-Luminosity Conditions
abstract: Signal superposition, also called pile-up noise, has become an important issue for calorimeters operating under high-luminosity conditions. Current techniques model those superimposed signals as highly correlated noise, whose covariance matrix information is used to develop a minimal variance and unbiased filter for energy estimation. Hence, the filter coefficients depend on the current luminosity. This paper presents a new energy estimator whose design is independent of the luminosity. The method consists of deconvolving the front-end electronic shaping response, recovering the primary impulse signals. The deconvolution process allows the identification of each superimposed component. Based on that information, it is possible to include additional constraints in order to design orthogonal estimators for each component, providing optimal estimators suitable to operate in colliders where luminosity changes during the run. Comparisons between current approaches and the proposed method using pile-up event simulation are presented. Results point out that an improvement on amplitude estimation performance is achieved and stable signal reconstruction is provided for different luminosity ranges.

title: An implementation of a Power System Smart Waveform Recorder using FPGA and ARM cores
abstract: In this paper, the design and the prototype implementation of a Power Quality (PQ) disturbance detector and compressor are described. This instrument, named Power System Smart Waveform Recorder (PSSWR), is able to acquire samples of Electric Power System (EPS) signals and process them to detect, compress, and store disturbance waveforms on an SD card, from which they can be reconstructed and analyzed offline using a suitable computer application. The prototype uses, among other devices, Field Programmable Gate Array (FPGA) and ARM platforms to process EPS signals in an intelligent way. The PSSWR is capable of detecting and recording both well-known PQ disturbance waveforms and new disturbances not yet observed in EPS, thanks to the Novelty Detection concept. This characteristic is important in the context of smart grids, where hidden disturbances can be identified using this methodology.

title: Gapless Power-Quality Disturbance Recorder
abstract: This paper presents and discusses the concepts of a gapless power quality disturbance recorder (G-PQDR) and a novelty detector. In the G-PQDR, power signals are divided into continuous frames, and the energy difference between a current frame and a reference frame is used for comparison. If the current frame differs from the reference frame, it is recorded and becomes the new reference; otherwise, the frame is discarded and only the frequency of the fundamental component is stored. The G-PQDR is capable of detecting and recording both previously known PQ disturbance waveforms and those not yet observed in power systems, thanks to the novelty detection concept. This feature is particularly important in the context of smart grids, where hidden disturbances can be identified using this methodology. To ensure efficient data storage by minimizing the volume of stored data, the G-PQDR employs both lossy and lossless compression techniques. This paper describes the complete process of detection and compression and compares the performance of the proposed method with that of a commercial power quality data recorder. Additionally, details of the prototype implementation are presented.

title: Sparse Representation for Signal Reconstruction in Calorimeters Operating in High Luminosity
abstract: A calorimeter signal reconstruction method based on sparse representation (SR) of redundant data is proposed for energy reconstruction in particle colliders operating under high-luminosity conditions. Signal overlapping is initially modeled as an underdetermined linear system, leading to a convex set of feasible solutions. The solution with the smallest number of superimposed signals (i.e., the sparse representation) that best represents the recorded data is obtained using an interior-point (IP) optimization procedure. From a signal processing perspective, this procedure performs source separation, enabling the estimation of the amplitude of each convoluted signal. Simulation results include a comparison between the proposed method and a standard signal reconstruction approach. For this purpose, a toy Monte Carlo simulation was developed, focusing exclusively on calorimeter front-end signal generation, where different levels of pile-up and signal-to-noise ratio were considered to evaluate the method. The results indicate that the proposed approach can be competitive in high-luminosity environments.

title: Embedded Signal Processing Module for Online Filtering in High-Event Rate Conditions
journal: Journal of Communication and Information Systems
abstract: Online event detection (filtering) is required in communications, industry, and electronic instrumentation systems, which may involve sequential decision levels. In some cases, the decision to reject or accept an event relies on the fusion of different measured data. This work describes an embedded solution implemented on a field-programmable gate array (FPGA), capable of combining information from two different sources to support decision-making. A matched filter discriminator is employed to identify signatures of interest. The main focus is on digital data control, packaging module design, and the implementation and optimization of the matched filter module in terms of FPGA resource utilization. The proposed digital electronic system is presented, and simulation results are used to validate the design.

title: Online energy reconstruction for calorimeters under high pile-up conditions using deconvolutional techniques
abstract: High-energy calorimeters operating at high data acquisition rates may suffer from signal superposition (pile-up) in their electronic readout. This issue arises when the time interval between two consecutive incoming physics signals is shorter than the front-end electronics latency. Signal pile-up can significantly degrade online energy estimation, and some palliative approaches treat pile-up as an additional noise source to preserve the well-established Optimal Filter theory as the baseline for signal estimation. To enable actual online compensation for pile-up effects, the method proposed in this paper models the front-end electronics as a linear transmission channel whose transfer function can be equalized (deconvolved). For experiments in which the acquisition rate is synchronized with the collision clock, the equalizer output allows direct energy estimation per bunch-crossing (BC). Optimal approximations for the equalizer design are proposed using both FIR (Finite Impulse Response) filters and an iterative method. The latter provides improved reconstruction performance (i.e., smaller errors), but at the cost of higher computational complexity and additional data-flow circuitry for online implementation. Toy simulation data, based on typical calorimeter front-end responses, are used to evaluate the performance of the proposed methods.

title: Sparse representation algorithm applied to power systems signal compression
journal: International Journal of Numerical Modelling: Electronic Networks, Devices and Fields
abstract: Power system signal compression is an important task due to the nature of electrical disturbances, which must be analyzed offline to extract useful information. This paper presents a comparative study of greedy algorithms for obtaining sparse representations of signals applied to power system signal compression. The algorithms are evaluated based on two parameters: the quality of the compressed signal, measured by its correlation coefficient relative to the original signal, and the number of elements in the representation, which is related to the compression ratio. Additionally, computational complexity, in terms of required floating-point operations (FLOPs), is analyzed to identify which algorithm is most suitable for real-time implementation. Finally, a comparison with the wavelet transform is performed using a database of real power system signals.

title: Performance Evaluation of Energy Reconstruction Methods in High Energy Physics Experiments
abstract: The discovery of particles that shape our universe drives the scientific community to develop increasingly sophisticated equipment. Particle accelerators are complex machines that bring particle beams into collision at speeds close to the speed of light. When these collisions occur, secondary products are generated and measured by calorimeter systems, which fully absorb them. Typically, high-energy calorimeters are highly segmented, comprising thousands of dedicated readout channels. This work evaluates the performance of two energy reconstruction algorithms: the Optimal Filter (OF) and the Multi-Amplitude Estimator (MAE), recently proposed to address signal superposition (pile-up). To assess energy estimation efficiency, artificial data are used under various pile-up conditions. Statistical analysis of the estimated energy is then employed to compare the performance of each method. A second analysis evaluates the sensitivity of the MAE to the pedestal parameter. The results show that the MAE method outperforms the OF method, although the use of an uncalibrated pedestal value negatively affects its performance.

title: A matched filter based approach for high-energy estimation in calorimetry
abstract: In high-energy calorimeters, a crucial task is the reconstruction of the energy deposited by particle interactions. Standard techniques used in modern calorimeters rely on energy estimation to select signals with relevant information. This work presents a new approach that performs signal detection in the presence of noise as a first step, followed by energy estimation. The method is fully based on Matched Filter (MF) theory, which is known to provide optimal detection efficiency with respect to the signal-to-noise ratio. Furthermore, the MF output can be calibrated to estimate the signal amplitude and, consequently, the energy. The proposed method is compared with different optimal filtering algorithms currently used for energy reconstruction in modern calorimeter systems. Simulation results show that the proposed approach achieves superior performance in terms of both signal detection efficiency and estimation error.

title: Linear Equation System Solver Circuit for Streaming Processing in FPGA
journal: Journal of Control, Automation and Electrical Systems
abstract: The solution of linear equation systems (LES) has numerous applications in digital signal processing. However, due to its high computational cost, implementing such solutions in high-speed, free-running (streaming) processing applications is challenging. This work presents an FPGA-based implementation of a digital circuit for solving LES using the gradient descent method, capable of operating online. This implementation introduces a new perspective for executing iterative, matrix-based algorithms in embedded systems designed for high-speed (tens of MHz) continuous data flow. In other words, the proposed method functions as a real-time processing block, where new data enters and processed data exits at each clock cycle, enabling seamless integration of matrix computations into continuous-flow systems. In addition to presenting the implementation architecture, this work analyzes the relationship between the number of iterations, resource utilization, and the achievable acquisition rate. As a case study, the method is applied to a channel equalization problem.

title: Sparse deconvolution methods for online energy estimation in calorimeters operating in high luminosity conditions
abstract: Energy reconstruction in calorimeters operating in high-luminosity particle colliders has become a significant challenge. In this scenario, pulses from the calorimeter front-end output overlap (pile-up effect), compromising energy estimation when no preprocessing for signal separation is applied. Recently, signal deconvolution-based methods have been proposed for both online and offline reconstruction. For online processing, constraints such as processing speed, memory requirements, and implementation cost limit overall performance. In contrast, offline reconstruction allows the use of Sparse Representation theory to implement more sophisticated iterative deconvolution methods. This paper presents iterative deconvolution approaches based on sparse representation algorithms with computational costs suitable for online implementation. Using simulated data, the proposed methods are compared with current techniques to evaluate performance in online environments. The analysis shows that, despite higher computational cost compared to standard methods, the performance gains may justify their use—particularly the Separable Surrogate Functional approach, which proves to be feasible for implementation on modern FPGAs.

title: Spectral Variation-Based Signal Compression Technique for Gapless Power Quality Waveform Recording in Smart Grids
abstract: The current scenario of electric power systems expansion requires the insertion of new devices and technologies, contributing to the establishment of smart grids. However, the more instruments are connected to the grid, the greater the amount of data resulting from the measurements, which can cause a bottleneck in data transferring. The present article addresses this problem by proposing a system that implements a novel and efficient real-time data compression method based on the power signal spectral content variation, focused on long-term gapless oscillographic analysis. This system also provides a high-fidelity reconstruction for full oscillography power quality analysis. Comparison results with synthetic and real signals using another gapless power quality disturbance recorder present in literature and a commercial power quality disturbance recorder show that the proposed technique is capable to achieve a compression rate more than ten times greater than both while providing a better reconstruction quality, reaching compression ratio values of up to 10780:1 for synthetic signals and 9470:1 for real signals.

title: Identification of Low Impedance Points Along Railway Tracks From a Railroad Inspection Vehicle
journal: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: This work addresses the subject of preventive maintenance of railroads which has a considerable impact on the cost and safety of railway companies by proposing a new technique to detect concentrated regions of low impedance between rails along railway lines from a railroad inspection vehicle. Its methodology is based on the standing waves theory applied to transmission lines. Simulation and field measurements have been carried out in order to understand and validate such technique, and a compact measurement system has been designed and implemented producing field-testing results that clearly indicate its feasibility and effectiveness.

title: Performance of Optimal Linear Filtering Methods for Signal Estimation in High-Energy Calorimetry
journal: Journal of Control, Automation and Electrical Systems
abstract: Discrete linear estimation methods, which are based on a weighted sum of the received time samples and the filter coefficients, are extensively employed in many applications. In conditions where the expected pulse shape is known and the received signal is mainly corrupted from additive Gaussian noise, such approaches perform close to the optimal operation. However, the readout signal may also suffer from pile-up, which potentially deteriorates the estimation process. In this paper, signal estimation is carried out for high-event rate experiments in high-energy physics, which aim at reconstructing particle collisions. In particular, the energies of the subproducts resulting from the collisions are measured by the calorimeter systems using shaped signals and estimating their amplitudes. The paper presents four linear estimation approaches and compares their performance in high signal pile-up conditions: two versions of an Optimal Filter that minimizes the noise variance impacts, a Wiener Filter to cope with the uncertainties arising from both the noise and the signal fluctuations, and a signal deconvolution technique that recovers the original signals within a calorimeter readout window. The efficiency is measured from simulated data where various signal pile-up scenarios and noise conditions are considered.

title: Probability based data quality for energy reconstruction in high energy calorimetry
journal: The Journal of Engineering and Exact Sciences
abstract: In several engineering applications, the quality of the reconstruction of the information of interest directly interferes with the success of decision making. In modern high-energy physics experiments, the reconstruction of events is essential for the precise observation of particles and important discoveries for science. In this context, the signals from the calorimetry system provide information about the energy of the particles produced in collisions. Typically, the energy reconstruction quality is measured using the χ² method. However, in experiments operating in high luminosity conditions, such an approach is not able to operate under signals pile-up conditions, being only able to detect the presence of pile-up effect in the received signal. Therefore, in this work, a new approach to evaluate the quality of energy reconstruction under pile-up conditions is presented. The proposed method relies on the calculation of probabilities regardless of the energy reconstruction algorithm employed. Using simulated data for a general calorimeter system operating under different signals pile-up conditions, the results show that the use of the proposed method increases signals selection efficiency.

title: An FPGA-based parallel deconvolution application envisaging high-energy calorimeters operating under severe pile-up conditions
abstract: Signal pileup may arise when the system response time is larger than what would be required by the event rate in the application. In this condition, information superposition occurs, and signal estimation is deteriorated due to such information distortion. For online applications, mitigating such a pileup distortion usually requires embedded digital signal processing, which often involves FPGA-based designs when high event rates are a concern. Recently, high-energy particle experiments started facing an unprecedented increase in pileup conditions, which demanded new approaches for signal estimation. This work proposes a dedicated parallel signal processing system embedded on an FPGA platform that implements different filtering techniques for online energy reconstruction in calorimeters (energy measurement) operating under severe pileup conditions. Each designed filter is an inverse approximation of the calorimeter readout channel’s impulse response, performing deconvolution of the incoming signals. Such a deconvolution approach for energy estimation was recently introduced, and the proposed filters were implemented in this work. Evaluation of the digital system design was performed using a data set that considers a typical high-energy calorimeter front-end response, as high-luminosity particle collider experiments produce high pileup distortions in calorimeter signals when processing the subproducts of the collisions. As the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) provides the most demanding conditions in terms of signal pileup, the acquisition system of the proposed solution is synchronous with its collision rate (40 MHz). The results showed that the proposed implementation may be applied as a preprocessing (pileup reduction) step in calorimeter instrumentation and could reduce the signal pileup within the fast response time required by such experiments.

title: Non-negative sparse deconvolution method for PMT signals in radiation detectors
journal: Nuclear Instruments and Methods in Physics Research
abstract: Electrical signals generated by radiation detectors are often affected by undesired effects that can degrade the detector’s energy resolution. These effects may originate from the measurement system itself or from high event rates. Traditional signal shaping and mitigation methods present limitations, such as the need for explicit prior information about the signals. This paper proposes a deconvolution method based on least squares filtering and non-negative sparsity theory to overcome these limitations. The method is evaluated using simulated data representing a gamma-ray detector composed of a NaI(Tl) crystal and a photomultiplier tube (PMT). In this system, the electrical pulses are preprocessed using a CR coupling circuit followed by an RC integrator. The results show that the traditional method achieves an energy resolution of 6.60%, whereas the proposed method improves it to 5.65%. In addition to increasing accuracy, the proposed approach also reduces implementation complexity.

title: A Maximum Likelihood Approach for Signal Estimation and Data Quality Assessment for High-Energy Calorimeter Systems
abstract: In high-energy calorimetry, energy estimation is typically performed using digital linear filters, from which the amplitude of a conditioned signal is inferred. The linear approach is justified when electronic noise can be approximated by a Gaussian distribution. However, calorimeters operating at high event rates and under high-luminosity conditions—such as those in modern particle colliders—are affected by non-Gaussian noise sources, notably signal tails from adjacent collisions that do not carry relevant physical information about the event of interest. Despite this, the true noise probability density function (pdf) is often neglected, and only second-order statistics are considered to preserve the linear model.This work presents a novel approach based on the full characterization of the noise distribution using maximum likelihood estimators (MLE). An iterative optimization algorithm is proposed to determine the parameter values that maximize a non-Gaussian multivariate distribution. Additionally, the study evaluates the use of maximum likelihood estimates for data quality assessment, enabling the rejection of signals with poor estimation quality. Simulation results comparing the proposed method with conventional linear approaches show that the non-Gaussian MLE achieves superior performance in terms of estimation error across a wide range of cell occupancy conditions. Furthermore, the final likelihood value can be used as a performance metric for signal reconstruction, providing a robust quality factor (QF) for discriminating signals in physics analyses when compared to traditional strategies.

title: Online Pulse Compensation for Energy Spectrum Determination: A Pole-Zero Cancellation and Unfolding Approach
abstract: Signal conditioning circuits in particle energy spectrum determination systems introduce shaping characteristics that affect pulse integrity. This study investigates algorithms to compensate for these effects, focusing on digital signal processing techniques for pole-zero cancellation (PZC) and unfolding. The PZC algorithm effectively corrects baseline shifts and pulse amplitude loss, significantly improving signal fidelity. Although the application of digital PZC in streaming mode for high event rates was previously considered impractical, this work proposes an FPGA-based implementation combined with the unfolding method, enabling online compensation and improved performance under various experimental conditions.

title: Learned Soft-Thresholding for Streaming Sparse Signal Recovery in Nuclear Instrumentation
abstract: Experiments in High Energy Physics, such as those conducted at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC), use calorimeters for particle detection and energy measurement. Under high-luminosity conditions, these systems face the pile-up effect, where overlapping signals distort energy estimation and reduce trigger efficiency. Sparse representation (SR)-based methods have been widely explored for signal deconvolution and pile-up mitigation in calorimeters. This paper proposes a novel deconvolution method that combines a finite impulse response (FIR) filter with sparse representation using a dynamic threshold estimated by a neural network. Unlike conventional SR approaches that rely on iterative algorithms—often unsuitable for real-time applications—the proposed method operates in a non-iterative, feed-forward manner, making it compatible with online data acquisition (DAQ) systems. Performance evaluations against traditional least squares (LS) techniques and SR methods with fixed thresholds demonstrate improved accuracy and resolution. Simulated datasets validate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, showing enhanced signal reconstruction while maintaining feasibility for hardware implementation.

title: Multi-Memory Approach for Random Number Generators in FPGA
abstract: Random number generation is essential in many application domains, including high-energy physics simulations. Implementing Monte Carlo methods that generate samples according to a desired probability distribution is particularly challenging on hardware platforms such as FPGAs. Direct implementations of analytical distribution functions are often resource-intensive, making them impractical for real-time systems. An efficient alternative is the use of the inverse cumulative distribution function (CDF), which can be implemented using look-up tables (LUTs). In this approach, a uniformly distributed random number—generated by linear feedback shift registers (LFSRs)—is used as an address to access LUTs containing discretized values of the CDF, thereby producing the desired random variable. However, this method presents limited accuracy in low-probability regions of the distribution. To address this limitation, this paper proposes a segmented CDF implementation based on multiple LUTs, improving resolution in poorly sampled regions. A cascade of decision logic is used to select the appropriate memory output, increasing resolution only where necessary while optimizing memory usage. The proposed method is validated through Monte Carlo simulations in particle physics applications, showing close agreement with theoretical distributions while requiring low FPGA resource utilization and no DSP blocks.

title: SAPHO—Scalable-Architecture Processor for Hardware Optimization: An FPGA Customizable Implementation Approach
abstract: Soft-core processors are increasingly used in field-programmable gate arrays (FPGAs) to implement complex functions at critical points in embedded systems, where a pure firmware solution would require excessive development effort or hardware resources. However, most existing processors rely on fixed architectures, allocating the same amount of logic resources regardless of application requirements. This often leads to hardware over-provisioning, oversized data word lengths, and unnecessary power consumption. To address these limitations, this work presents SAPHO, a self-scalable processor that automatically adjusts its program and data memories, as well as its arithmetic and logic circuits, according to software requirements. This design paradigm reduces hardware waste, lowers logic element utilization, and enables more energy-efficient execution compared to conventional soft-core processors. Additionally, SAPHO incorporates a combinational Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU), configurable for fixed- or floating-point operations, ensuring the execution of one instruction per clock cycle without pipeline stalls. Performance evaluation shows that, compared to the widely used commercial soft-core processor NIOS II, SAPHO achieves lower hardware cost and shorter execution time while maintaining scalability and flexibility for different embedded applications. Experimental results indicate that SAPHO executes all arithmetic operations in a fixed sequence of 13 clock cycles, whereas equivalent operations on the NIOS II may require from tens to thousands of cycles depending on configuration and data type. In an 8-point FFT benchmark, SAPHO reduces execution time by 77.36% compared to the fastest NIOS II configuration. Furthermore, SAPHO requires fewer logic elements (LEs) and demonstrates lower dynamic core power consumption, with reductions ranging from 15% to 33% compared to NIOS II variants. These results confirm that SAPHO provides a more efficient solution in terms of hardware cost, performance, and energy consumption while preserving flexibility for embedded applications.
